title: NOC 85120 – Logging and forestry labourers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 85120 represents the hardworking manual workforce that supports Canada’s massive timber and forest conservation industries. As a TEER 5 occupation, these labourers perform a variety of physical tasks to assist in felling, transporting logs, and maintaining healthy forest ecosystems. In 2026, as Canada balances high timber demand with urgent wildfire mitigation and reforestation goals, these labourers are the "boots on the ground" ensuring the sector remains productive and sustainable. For immigration, this role is a vital entry point for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the New 2026 TEER 5 PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level5Major Group85 – Harvesting, landscaping and natural resources labourersSub-Major Group851 – Harvesting, landscaping and natural resources labourersMinor Group8511 – Mine and oil and gas drilling, services and related labourersNOC Code85120

duties: Log Transport Support: Attach chokers (cables) to felled logs so they can be hauled by tractors or yarders to loading areas.
Clearance: Help clear brush and debris from logging sites, trails, and firebreaks to ensure crew safety and environmental compliance.
Sorting & Grading: Assist in sorting, piling, and loading logs onto trucks or into water booms for transport to mills.
Maintenance: Clean and maintain logging equipment, chain saws, and camp facilities; assist in minor mechanical repairs.
Silviculture Support: Assist in planting seedlings and applying fertilizers or chemicals to promote new forest growth.
Safety & Fire Watch: Monitor work sites for fire hazards and assist in forest fire suppression activities when required.
employment_requirements: No formal education is strictly required, though some secondary school is preferred by large forestry firms.
On-the-Job Training: A short period of training (1–4 weeks) is provided by the employer.

Certifications:First Aid Certification is mandatory for all workers in remote woodland operations.WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) for handling oils and chemicals.Chain Saw Safety training is often provided on-site.
First Aid Certification is mandatory for all workers in remote woodland operations.
WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) for handling oils and chemicals.
Chain Saw Safety training is often provided on-site.
Physical stamina, strength (ability to lift 25kg+), and the ability to work outdoors in all weather conditions (snow, rain, heat) are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Total annual earnings for camp-based workers often exceed $55,000 - $70,000 due to consistent overtime and remote site premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), the forestry sector is facing a "demographic cliff" with over 30% of the manual workforce eligible for retirement by 2030. Furthermore, the federal government's "2 Billion Trees" initiative has created a sustained multi-year demand for support labourers in the silviculture and reforestation sub-sectors.
labour_market_demand: The demand for logging and forestry labourers is Very Good, particularly in Canada’s "Timber Belt." In 2026, British Columbia, New Brunswick, and Northern Ontario are reporting critical labor shortages in woodland operations. While high-tech machinery (NOC 83110) handles much of the heavy felling, manual labourers are increasingly needed for Selective Harvesting and Fuel Management (removing brush to prevent forest fires), which cannot be done by machines alone.
High Accessibility: No formal education is required, allowing international workers to enter the Canadian workforce quickly.
Adventure & Remote Living: Ideal for those who enjoy working in Canada’s pristine wilderness and living in well-equipped forest camp environments.
Rapid Career Growth: This is the primary training ground for becoming a Chain Saw/Skidder Operator (NOC 84110) or a Silviculture Worker (NOC 84111).
Essential Industry Status: Forestry workers are often eligible for specialized work permit processing and regional PR streams due to their role in the national economy.
pathways: New 2026 TEER 4 & 5 Pathway: A newly launched federal stream targeting essential workers with at least one year of Canadian work experience.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-priority route for workers with job offers from designated forestry employers in NB, NS, PEI, or NL.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Accessible for those working in the province's forestry sector for at least 9 months.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Targeted at workers moving to forestry-dependent communities in Northern Ontario and Western Canada.
